problem_addressed: Fundamental limitations of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) under misspecification, specifically how systematic bias on a subset of contexts leads to a persistent performance gap that cannot be overcome with bounded query budgets.
core_claim: The performance gap always wins under misspecification—any learner using a bounded number of queries will face a gap scaling exponentially with queries, unless a calibration oracle identifies misspecified contexts.
key_contributions: Lower bound on performance gap for any learner: ≥γ/4 * exp(-nακ(ε)) with κ(ε) = 4ε atanh(2ε)
Upper bound with calibration oracle: Achieves gap ≤η with Q ≤ (1/(2αε²)) log(γ/η) queries
Tilted calculus framework to analyze optimization drift and distribution shift sensitivity
novelty: Precise quantification of misspecification impact via separation parameter γ and hard mass α, demonstrating exponential query dependence on α and ε, and identifying calibration oracles as critical for mitigating gaps.
method_summary: Uses Le Cam's two-point method with transcript-level KL decomposition for lower bounds, and majority testing on flagged contexts for upper bounds, combined with tilted calculus to analyze optimization dynamics.
method_components: Le Cam's two-point method and KL divergence for lower bound derivation
Majority test on flagged contexts for query-efficient gap reduction
Tilted sampling law qλ and covariance analysis for optimization drift
key_innovation: Identification that hard mass α and bias ε govern sample complexity, and that calibration oracles enable logarithmic query scaling via context routing—transforming exponential to polynomial complexity.
assumptions: Contexts drawn i.i.d. from distribution µ over X
Existence of measurable Xhard with mass α where feedback is biased
Pairwise label favors better action with prob 1/2 + wε on Xhard (ε ∈ (0, 1/2))
Separation parameter γ > 0 defined as expected reward difference on Xhard
For upper bound: Access to calibration oracle h(x) = 1{x ∈Xhard}
theoretical_grounding: Lower bound via Le Cam's method and KL decomposition; upper bound via Bernoulli KL and testing bounds; tilted calculus provides covariance-based drift analysis for optimization dynamics.
title: Murphys Laws of AI Alignment: Why the Gap Always Wins
